text: The surgeon places eight and twelve millimeter ports in a straight line parallel to the recto sigmoidal junction, starting two finger breaths from the superior anterior iliac spine, spaced seven centimeters apart. An additional twelve millimeter assistant port is positioned in triangulation between R-three and R-four ports. The operating table is tilted into Trendelenburg and right tilt positions. The omentum is positioned above the transverse colon, and the small bowel is moved to the right hemiabdomen to identify the trides ligament. Robotic arms are docked, and the target anatomy is inputted into the robot.

The surgeon opens the peritoneum in a broad front, two centimeters above the sacral promontory, allowing pneumoperitoneum to track behind the incised peritoneum and highlight dissection planes along the posterior side of the inferior mesenteric artery. Care is taken to avoid the hypogastric nerve and left common iliac artery and vein. The dissection proceeds around the inferior mesenteric artery and then to the inferior mesenteric vein, identifying the embryological plane connecting the two vessels. The incision under the inferior mesenteric vein allows air to dissect tissues, aiding in identifying correct planes while avoiding nearby retroperitoneal structures such as gonadal vessels, ureter, and hypogastric nerves.

The surgeon reaches the inferior mesenteric artery and creates a natural window inferior to the left colic branch. Three Hem-o-lok clips are placed under the junction of the left colic artery to ligate the artery.

The surgeon carefully prepares around the inferior mesenteric vein, applying hemlock clips for ligation while avoiding injury to the nearby duodenum. Medial to lateral mobilization continues with identification and dissection of the pancreas from the mesocolon. Traction is applied to reveal Toldt's fascia, and meticulous dissection develops the embryological planes, guided by small capillaries that end abruptly. The dissection progresses until the mesocolon is completely separated from Toldt's fascia, reaching the descending colon.

A swab is positioned over the pancreas to protect it and serve as a marker during the lateral dissection. The surgeon continues the lateral dissection, reconnecting with the previously dissected medial plane, until the swab is reached. Upon reaching the splenic flexure, the lienocolic and phrenocolic ligaments are dissected. The omentum is then separated from its colonic attachments to access the lesser sac, with traction applied by lifting the omentum towards the spleen to prevent injury to the splenic capsule.

The assistant provides counter traction by pulling the colon inferiorly, while the surgeon carefully dissects between the fatty tissues of the mesocolon and omentum to access the lesser sac. The previously placed swab over the pancreas serves as a marker, aiding in the completion of splenic flexure mobilization. The omental attachments of the transverse colon are separated, and the lesser sac is entered, freeing the splenic flexure completely.
